item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like having the guy say literally "we want to be part of the in-group" is rather on the nose. I also wish that there had been more exploration of the idea that what made them monsters was inside of them all along was less a result of their external monstrosity and more of their choosing to go along with hypernationalist militancy.

Still, this is a decent article, and it definitely breaks away from the bog-standard "spooky cronenberg lovecraft bastardmans + things that happened infinity billion years ago" deal that mark a lot Sarkic articles. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At first it didn't impressed me. Then I kept reading and I loved it.

I never liked the apocalyptic tone of most old skips and GOIs, it is rather unnecessary. And I like skips that make sarkicism into something more complex, nuanced, without denying it a simple core. This was very well written, describing the anomaly quickly and then focusing on giving it context in a beautiful way. There was no doubt in the upvote.

My own headcannon was nearing this approach already for some time. My interpretation on Sarkicism is that it is much more a non-anomalous cultural phenomenon that relies to an extend on anomalous tools they have. So it have anomalies, but it isn't the anomalies.

That includes that other nuances can and do exist outside the classic depictions of proto- and neo-sarkicism. To begin with, we have already a clear depiction of ancient sarkicism. A religion similar in several regards to others developing at the time, I even picture Nadox as involved in the theological discussions that resulted in Zoroastrianism, maybe even having encountered Zarathustra in person. This religion becomes the state religion of Ady-üm, and deifies its anomalous emperor, linking him to a messianic figure, but otherwise Ady-üm isn't too different from other city-states of the period.

Any Karcist could be responsible for 2478, but as anomalous as purported to be, Ion could very well be the one, indeed. It actually makes little difference. Proto-Sarkism is techno-phobic due to historical isolation, but there is no mention of something like that in the tales of Ady-üm. So it isn't illogical to think of some off-shot with the community sense of proto-sarkism, but more aligned to modern society.
This will be the third SCP of mine to have reached +100 in rating. My humble appreciations to all who have supported it. This is one that I am proud of.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Did you accidentally a verb?

Amended accordingly.

Is this intentional, SCP-2478-01 speaking about itself in plural?

Yes. Each instance originated from seven different people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Approximately 50 deceased specimens for autopsy

Did you accidentally a verb?

We are an ordinary person!

Is this intentional, SCP-2478-01 speaking about itself in plural?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You know why I like this?
It's a Sarkic SCP that isn't cartoonishly malevolent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If they could breed, they will still give birth to normal humans. They are, after all, normal Homo sapiens.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If SCP-2478 instances were able to breed, would it be necessary for them to produce new instances? I think it would be interesting if they just gave birth to normal humans… normal Japanese humans, who are bewildered and outraged at the way foreigners treat their perfectly normal parents and grandparents.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Does it bother them that they can't reproduce? (normal people usually have children, and these beings desperately want to be perceived as normal.)

Originally, I wanted them to reproduce normal humans who will become more instances through a ritual. But I ended up getting rid of it since it was needlessly complicated.

I do think they are not bothered by it. Wion's message of salvation keeps them opiated. And I imagine those villagers to be especially desperate and oppressed, even by the standards of the burakumin.

Also, if they were created in 1879 and can't breed, does that mean they live longer than normal humans?

Since they are made from seven humans, they actually have seven times the normal human lifespan.

And why are they classed Euclid? The Foundation seems to have a pretty good understanding of their anomalous properties and has them contained quite securely. Seems like they should be Safe to me.

In my personal rubric, if it is sentient, sapient and can move about to fight back, it should be Euclid by default. While they are currently contained safely, if Ion comes back, God knows what they may do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I find the SCP itself to be kind of underwhelming, I love your writing style. It's great the way you namedrop all these historical places and events and believably integrate the Foundation fiction into them. And the little references to other weird ideas are the best - the fact that the Foundation was kicked out of of Japan for harassing anomalous people that only non-Japanese can perceive as anomalous, or that the Japanese army had a "Youkai Battalion" - both of those would make amazing subjects for a tale.

As for the actual SCP, I feel that their psychology needs to be explored in more depth. Does each instance have all of the memories of the seven people that were used to create it? Do their gigantic brains make them smarter than normal people? Since most of them are presumably hermaphrodites, do they still have a cultural concept of gender? Does it bother them that they can't reproduce? (normal people usually have children, and these beings desperately want to be perceived as normal.)

Also, if they were created in 1879 and can't breed, does that mean they live longer than normal humans?

And why are they classed Euclid? The Foundation seems to have a pretty good understanding of their anomalous properties and has them contained quite securely. Seems like they should be Safe to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Some Japanese language trivia: If we transcribe "Wion" into Japanese hiragana, it would be 「ゐおん」(or wi-o-n).

The wi kana (ゐ) is mostly obsolete in modern Japanese, and is usually rendered as い (or i kana).

As such, "Wion" would be an anarchic way of writing "Ion".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't really like the idea that Ion himself was there and made it.

Hmm, the instances definitely knew the name 'Wion' (Ion), but that might not necessarily mean that Ion was physically present. Whatever came to that village was definitely a powerful Sarkicist, no matter how we see it. And Ion's name was spoken during that time.

An analogy would be RL Buddhism. Monks and practitioners would call upon buddhas and the like for guidance, but that does not mean that those figures are physically there. Some stories even have buddhas and the like disguised as local divinities, as they bless their followers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You'd think Ion would give the eta a form that'd allow them to violently overthrow the upper classes that torment them.

A rather ambiguous scenario, won't you think so. Proto-Sarkicists tend to play the long game, carrying the hope of paradise. Of course, Ion himself launched a full-scale rebellion in his life. So there is quite a contrast since then.

Perhaps the Grand Karcist wizened over the centuries? Perhaps it was not really Ion, but a (benevolent) aspect of him projected onto the world temporarily or a Karcist masquerading as him and using a name similar to his?

Almost because I find it hard to sympathize with anyone who volunteered to serve in the Japanese army during the Sino-Japanese war.

In retrospect, I find the part on 2478 serving the IJA to be a major element of the story here. Dr Solo thought of this as an analogy for Japan in World War II, which is not my intention. That said, after I posted it, I can see how it can be interpreted as such.

By serving the IJA, 2478 serves to engage in actions for the sake of the in-group (Japanese society at large). But the price for that is to literally become monstrous, and be monstrous in actions too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You'd think Ion would give the eta a form that'd allow them to violently overthrow the upper classes that torment them. That they were made instead into something the rest of their society would find useful and contributing is very… well, Japanese.

It's an almost sympathetic take on karcism. Almost because I find it hard to sympathize with anyone who volunteered to serve in the Japanese army during the Sino-Japanese war.

Solid upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For the sake of fairness, that will be quite a saga.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I might post this theory over in her thread, because it totally changes her entire motivations and why certain things tick her off so badly, especially if she actually had some level of affection for whichever emperor she tried to seduce first. Its not because she's petty; its because it brings back a lot of bad memories of trying not to be a monster and getting punished for it.

item.title: +1 BTW
item.content:encoded:
The mental image for how this played out is hilarious:

+ show block
– hide block

"Oh great prophet Ion, please help us."

"What do you desire?"

"Our people, they look down upon us, they say we are only one seventh of a person, each, and we are little better than slaves."

"One seventh a human, you say? I've got just the thing!"

"What, oh great prophet?"

"Come closer." <Horrific squelching noise> "There, problem solved."


At this point, my headcanon is that horrific body mutation is the sarkic equivalent of duct tape.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … Well, there is another myth that Tamamo-no-Mae (the fox spirit who seduced a certain Japanese emperor) was also Su Daji once upon a time (another fox spirit who brought the collapse of Shang Dynasty)… so yeah.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this for what it does for IJAMEA. I remember seeing the name before (as you pointed out), but it never really seemed like an entity in itself, like a GoI or anything. The whole "Foundation was expelled from Japan" thing brings some cool backstory to them and the world as a whole.

Also, the Sarkic elements were unexpected but kinda tie everything together nicely. Everything else has already been said.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wanna see a Foundation shooter game wherein the player character is Japanese and has to fight one of these. Imagine, just a bunch of katanas and WWII weapons floating in mid-air, surrounding a grotesque giant?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The former was my thinking, and now headcanon: she gets run out of Korea and heads over to Japan for a while ruining the reputation of the less homicidal Kitsune. Eventually she gets ambitious and tries to seduce the emperor, gets ghostbustered by the scholar, and takes a century or two to escape the warded jar labeled "Evil Kitsune".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't really like the idea that Ion himself was there and made it. Other than that, I think it's a pretty good SCP, even before I read about the 1 eta is 1/7 of a person thing. Any SCP which is both Sarkic and Antimemetic will probably be upvoted by me.

Also, Ion or not, thet Karcist must have had a wicked sense of humor. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Did 953 have a weeaboo phase that she really wants to forget? Or maybe she was originally Japanese but became a Koreaboo who actually moved to Korea and successfully integrated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I do like this. However, I'm pretty sure that this isn't antimemetic. Given that an antimeme is generally accepted to be an idea that resists being spread (usually by erasing information or forcing itself to be forgotten), this is just a memetic effect: the idea that it's just a person with gigantism, informed by the cultural information present in a consciously japanese person.

That's my prerequisite nitpick, +1 otherwise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this one a lot. I think i saw an earlier version in which the Sarkic element was more implied rather than spelled out (ditto with the 1/7 cultural explanation), and I found it kind of irritating at the time. This benefited a lot from spelling it out, making the connection and context clearer.

This ends up telling a story very similar to the (Series 1 I think?) SCP about the eastern European village that contracts tons of diseases and has a weird religion involving something horrible underneath it. I think I prefer this one, because it centers on weird, weird positivity and the unexpected finding of kindred spirits rather than just weirdness and horror. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Then I guess I'll use the site of first encountering them for the map

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, she's Korean. Couldn't have been her.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man, 953 gets around…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sarkism: Making twisted mockeries of nature for the greater good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A fictional village that no longer exists, I had some name ideas for it that I settled in favour of blackboxes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What's the name of the village?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
History is the best writer.

See this, right here, kids? This is why we do our research.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not just because it uses my GOI (and gives it a better name)

Don't thank me. Thank the Japanese. The Bureau of Onmyo is a real group in Heian Japan, but their official duties were mainly calendar making and astrology. That said, there was a famous legend of an onmyo master who caught an evil fox spirit seducing the emperor.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really have to stop mistyping stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: psst. MrWrong, I think Smapti wrote 1845.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Collapsed because spoilers 
– hide block

I like this. I like it a lot. Not just because it uses my GOI (and gives it a better name), but because I'm really, really into moral ambiguity, and this shows Ion as being a really, really good guy. The idea of an entire caste of people who are rejected being given something that frees them from this rejection but doesn't make them into bullies and monsters[citation needed] seems exactly like something a benevolent god would do. The difference between this Sarkicism and most depictions of it is like the difference between Christan "fire and brimstone" sermon and a Christian "Jesus is forgiving" sermon. There being a spectrum of beliefs influenced by Ion makes more sense than the typical Sarkicism, and the cool thing is, at the end of the day, this doesn't even nullify evil Sarkicists.

GG, MrWrong.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
An allusion to the anomalous animals that practice Catholicism. I figured they are the best well-known case of SCPs who practice a religion and get to practice it.

That said, I reckon that SCP-1845 was found in the 2000s and this is definitely in the 20th century. But fuck that, if there is SCP-2000, timelines make no difference.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like we're beginning to see a kinder, gentler side to Sarkicism.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You had me at "Eugenio II protocol".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for the lookout. I always miss them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Got a couple of "SCP-#" still in there…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I loved this, very clever with the whole 1/7th of a person thing

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Like do they notice their differences from other humans?

They kinda do, but they don't care.

How are their lives hampered?

It took some time to get adjusted, but they managed to work things out (even fighting in China in WWII).

Did they breed with other humans?

Nah, they are sterile. That said, I did originally have them breed babies internally, since some instances were made from a mix of men and women.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Would've liked to see more characterization of the entities and maybe more about their plight with their physical condition.

Put an interview to address their POV. But in summary, they just don't care — Thye just want to be not discriminated and be treated as part of the in-group.

Sarkicism link seemed a bit contrived.

I hope the interview covered that up to some extent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
festival is dedicated to Wion

resemblance to Sarkic rites

Wion

ion

I see what you did there

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It might be because I am part Japanese but I do like the concept for this article. However I also agree with Rogert, it feels a little lacking. Maybe an interview log or two to expand on it? Like do they notice their differences from other humans? How are their lives hampered? Did they breed with other humans? A lot of unanswered questions that could lead to some good detail to flesh out their distorted figure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the good use of history to support a so-so effect. Would've liked to see more characterization of the entities and maybe more about their plight with their physical condition. The bits included about their culture could be used for that. Sarkicism link seemed a bit contrived. Overall, I liked where it was going but I think you could do more with the material. no-vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As I said before, I don't dig the concept, but the writing is on point, just personal taste.

No-Vote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I adore this article. +1

item.title: Thanks, Attributions and Other Matters
item.content:encoded:
Firstly, it is thanks delivered to those who reviewed it as a draft. That's to Solo, Steeltitan, DrStranger, salvagebar and GuardGoldman.

Secondly, some attribution notes:

  • IJAMEA was from SCP-2937 by Mazyrian
  • Holy Emperor's Council on Unearthly Matters is taken from Documeny 2237 Bravo by MacLeod. Bureau of Onmyo is my take on it, since MacLeod's name for the Japanese precursor was tangential at best in my headcanon.
  • Sarkicism Hub by Metaphysician

For other things:

In an 1859 court case described by author Shimazaki Toson, a magistrate declared that "An eta is worth 1/7 of an ordinary person."

+ show block
– hide block

Therefore, seven eta should be one ordinary person, no?

This is a different take on Sarkicism. We've seen enough of the incoming apocalypse and body horror they will bring to the world, but Ion likely started all this with good intentions (freeing humanity from Daevite rule).

Likewise, the burakumin are the oppressed and for reasons unknown to the SCP Foundation, he gave those butchers and gravediggers a miracle. After all, extremism is often paved with good intentions.
